@graph.name: Land Rover Defender 300Tdi Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender 300Tdi engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 300Tdi engine typically costs £2,800–£4,200 supply only, with used units available from £1,000–£2,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Defender.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender 300Tdi engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the 300Tdi is widely considered the most reliable Land Rover engine ever produced. Its purely mechanical Bosch fuel injection system has no ECU to fail, and examples regularly exceed 250,000–500,000 miles with basic maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Defender 300Tdi?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing belt wear (replace every 60,000–72,000 miles), P-gasket coolant leaks between the water pump housing and block, and vacuum pump failure. All are well-understood and straightforward to repair by any Land Rover specialist.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender 300Tdi engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and chassis condition. Against a cherished, rust-free Defender worth £10,000+, a £3,500 recon is just 35% — well worth it. Against a tired example with significant chassis corrosion, the 60% Land Rover threshold suggests getting a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender 300Tdi engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: All 300Tdi engines carry the same engine code — '300Tdi'. Production years for UK-market Defenders were 1994–1998, with military and export production continuing to 2006. If your Defender is a 1994–1998 model with a 2.5-litre turbodiesel, it's almost certainly a 300Tdi.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 300Tdi timing belt, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — never attempt to repair a worn timing belt. A preventative replacement every 60,000–72,000 miles (£400–£800) is far cheaper than a snapped belt requiring a full engine rebuild or replacement (£2,800+). At the first sign of ticking from the front of the engine, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Defender 300Tdi Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current UK market for the Classic Defender 300Tdi. This badge used exactly one engine throughout its history — no era ambiguity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Defender 300Tdi engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Professional workshop install costs from £1,500+ VAT.

cta.label: → Compare 300Tdi engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Belt Wear and Failure
problems.symptoms: Ticking from front of engine, rough running, sudden loss of power, engine stops. Belt tension set incorrectly leads to short belt life and potential injection pump damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if belt fails, the engine stops instantly and internal damage is highly likely

problems.recommendation: This is the single most critical maintenance item on the 300Tdi. Land Rover recommends replacement at 72,000 miles, but many specialists advise 60,000 miles for peace of mind. Changing the timing belt on these engines is easier than on many cars and well within the capabilities of a competent DIY mechanic — but if you're not confident, get a specialist to do it. A snapped belt means a full engine rebuild or replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing belt failure guide

problems.title: P-Gasket and Water Pump Leaks
problems.symptoms: Gradual coolant loss, rising engine temperature, dampness around the front of the engine block, overheating on long journeys
problems.recommendation: The gasket between the water pump housing and block (the "P-gasket") is a known weak point and prone to leaking. It is vital to check coolant levels weekly and before any long journey. Left unchecked, coolant loss leads to overheating and potential cylinder head damage. Catch it early and it's a straightforward, relatively inexpensive repair.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: Vacuum Pump Failure
problems.symptoms: Hard brakes, hissing noise from engine bay, oil leak from the front of the engine
problems.recommendation: The 300Tdi's vacuum pump is a common failure point as mileage accumulates. It runs off the front of the engine and provides vacuum for the brake servo. When it fails, you lose power-assisted braking — which is alarming in a 2+ tonne Defender. Replacement is straightforward and parts are readily available from LR specialists.

failureLink.label: Read the full vacuum pump failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Defender 300Tdi? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented timing belt change within the last 60,000 miles or 5 years
* No signs of coolant loss around the water pump housing (P-gasket area)
* Full service history with regular oil and filter changes
* A well-maintained example with honest mileage — these engines are known to exceed 250,000+ miles
avoidIf: * No proof of timing belt replacement — this is the single biggest risk on the 300Tdi
* Signs of coolant loss, overheating, or "mayo" under the oil filler cap
* A tired example where the cost of a reconditioned engine (£2,800+) exceeds the vehicle's value
* Poor service history — this engine is forgiving but not indestructible

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 300Tdi is one of the most straightforward engines to own — no ECU, no DPF, no EGR complications. It rewards good maintenance with exceptional longevity. The timing belt is the one non-negotiable item; everything else is simple, mechanical, and well-understood by UK Land Rover specialists. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and this engine will outlast the chassis.

cta.label: → Compare 300Tdi engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Defender 300Tdi (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Timing Belt History — Has it been changed within the last 60,000 miles? Any receipt?
2. Service History — including regular oil changes (every 6,000 miles recommended)
3. Coolant Check — Any signs of P-gasket leaks or coolant loss?
4. Cold Start — Does it start cleanly from cold? Any excessive smoke?
5. Oil Condition — Black is normal; "mayo" under the cap means head gasket trouble
6. Vacuum Pump — Brakes firm? Any hissing from the front of the engine?
7. Engine Warning Light — There isn't one — that's the beauty of mechanical injection
8. MOT History — Check for corrosion-related failures (chassis, not engine)
